title: The Roast

jokes.text: David accuses his friends of 'general merriment' and they react with mock-disbelief at the word
jokes.setup: David walks in on Patrick and Stevie laughing and immediately adopts his mother Moira's theatrical vocabulary
jokes.punchline: Patrick and Stevie call out the word 'merriment' as absurdly formal; David blames his mother for the verbal contagion
jokes.explanation: Works beautifully because it's deeply character-specific: David has been infected by Moira's florid vocabulary and he knows it. The self-awareness ('I blame you two for it') makes it land. Strong character integration — this could only be David.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Why do you talk like that?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, David. It's weird.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My mother raised me to have a certain level of eloquence and refinement in my speech patterns.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: See? There it is again.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not my fault. Blame Rose.

jokes.text: She told me to 'dance like an Indonesian scarf caught in the wind.'
jokes.setup: Moira is described as a demanding director giving acting/dance notes
jokes.punchline: Her direction is so surreally specific and useless that Stevie doesn't even know what it looks like
jokes.explanation: This is a standout line. The specificity of 'Indonesian scarf' is pure Moira — geographically exotic, completely impractical as direction, and delivered without irony by Moira (off-screen). Stevie's follow-up 'I don't even know what that looks like' is the perfect straight-man button. The joke captures Moira's character perfectly without her even being present. Competes with the best jokes of the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Now, when you're moving through space, I want you to think of yourself as a delicate chrysalis unfurling in reverse. Not backwards, darling—that would be pedestrian. I want you to move as though you're a thought that Cocteau had and then immediately forgot.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm not sure I understand what that means.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course you don't. That's precisely why you need my direction. Now, again—but this time, imagine you're a silk scarf caught in a gentle but artfully tragic wind.

jokes.text: David to Patrick: 'You have legs like tree trunks, we knew that going into this.' Patrick replies: 'Thank you.'
jokes.setup: Patrick says his dancing is so bad they'll be laughed off stage
jokes.punchline: David casually insults Patrick's legs as a known, accepted fact; Patrick's sincere 'Thank you' treats it as a compliment
jokes.explanation: The two-beat delivery is the key: David's insult is delivered as plain fact, and Patrick's cheerful 'Thank you' reframes it as a compliment. Their relationship dynamic — Patrick's warmth vs David's bluntness — makes it sing. Economical and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, which way is the scarf supposed to go?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's an Indonesian scarf, Patrick. It goes... Indonesian.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Right, but what does that mean, exactly?

jokes.text: 'What is this, Dirty Dancing?!'
jokes.setup: Patrick and Stevie reveal they couldn't 'nail the final lift' with their secret dance coach
jokes.punchline: David's incredulous pop-culture comparison, as if he's caught them in a movie cliché
jokes.explanation: Functional pop-culture reference that earns a chuckle. The setup (lifting) perfectly justifies the Dirty Dancing callback. Somewhat lazy in terms of originality but the timing and David's delivery elevate it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, if it helps, I don't think anyone will laugh at you. I think they'll just sit in silence, which is somehow worse.

jokes.text: Ted reveals he already made brunch for them, Alexis says she already ate — he says he'll just have her plate for dinner. Alexis: 'Yum!'
jokes.setup: Ted made brunch they were supposed to cook together; Alexis casually announces she already ate
jokes.punchline: Ted's resigned passive-aggression ('I'll just have your plate for dinner') met with Alexis's completely oblivious 'Yum!'
jokes.explanation: Efficient character comedy: Ted's gentle martyrdom and Alexis's cheerful obliviousness are perfectly captured in a brief exchange. 'Yum!' as an enthusiastic endorsement of cold leftovers is a small gem.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You can't just bring in some random celebrity to give pageant advice. Do you know what happened the last time someone did that? Neither do I, because it's never happened, but I'm sure it was a disaster.

jokes.text: Ted keeps steering the conversation toward 'the Galapagos Islands' while Alexis keeps enthusiastically redirecting to other destinations
jokes.setup: Ted wants to propose the Galapagos trip but can't get Alexis to land on it
jokes.punchline: He says 'Galapagos Islands' twice, explicitly, and Alexis enthusiastically agrees to 'an island, somewhere' — never catching the hint
jokes.explanation: The escalating frustration as Ted says 'Galapagos Islands' verbatim and Alexis still doesn't hear it is a well-constructed comedic bit. It also functions as plot setup. Alexis landing on 'I'm so glad we're on the same page' after completely missing the point is an excellent button.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: There's so much wildlife in Miami.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, yeah. The nightlife there is insane.

jokes.text: Roland effusively praises Moira's roast ability: 'You better have the burn unit on speed dial... she is just slinging heat!'
jokes.setup: Roland laments that Moira won't be at the roast tonight
jokes.punchline: Roland escalates the fire metaphor to absurd heights, piling on 'burn unit,' 'fire extinguishers,' and 'slinging heat' — Johnny's deadpan 'Yeah, I get it' deflates the whole thing
jokes.explanation: Roland's oblivious over-extension of the metaphor is solid character comedy, and Johnny's weary 'I get it' is a good straight-man deflation. The joke is a bit long for what it delivers.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: So I was thinking we could go to the Galapagos Islands for our honeymoon.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, yes, totally. I was thinking more like... Paris? Or maybe the Maldives?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Right, so Galapagos. We're on the same page.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Completely on the same page. I'm seeing Bora Bora, obviously.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Galapagos.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm, Bora Bora.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: We're so good at communicating.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: The best. I'll start looking at flights to... you know where.

jokes.text: Johnny's first roast attempt at Roland: 'Well, maybe you'd know [what rippers are], if you graduated elementary school.'
jokes.setup: Roland says he has no idea what 'rippers' are
jokes.punchline: Johnny snaps back with an elementary-school graduation jab — his first successful roast line
jokes.explanation: It's a fairly standard insult but in context — Johnny finally landing a zinger in practice — it works. The surprise of Johnny actually pulling it off is more funny than the line itself.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I've been talking about the Galapagos for weeks now.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, and I think Cabo would be so much better for us.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: The Galapagos.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Or maybe Turks and Caicos?

jokes.text: Roland's stunned reaction: 'I can't believe you said that to me.' — followed by Johnny's immediate guilt: 'Was that too far?'
jokes.setup: Johnny lands his first roast burn on Roland
jokes.punchline: Roland acts genuinely wounded, and Johnny immediately backpedals with concern — until Roland reveals he was playing along
jokes.explanation: The beat between the zinger and Roland's faux-wounded reaction is well-timed. Johnny's anxious 'Was that too far?' reveals his roasting insecurity perfectly. The subversion — Roland was testing him — adds a layer.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I miss Moira. She's like a fire that burns so bright, consuming everything in its path, leaving nothing but ash and devastation in her wake. A fire that spreads across the dry plains of my heart, engulfing every rational thought, every moment of peace, until I'm nothing but a smoldering heap of longing and regret. A fire that—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, she's been gone for three days.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: A FIRE, David!

jokes.text: Roland mocks Johnny by repeating his apologetic tone back at him: 'Look at your face! Oh Roland, I'm sorry, did I go too far?'
jokes.setup: Johnny collapses into apology after his roast line
jokes.punchline: Roland gleefully mimics Johnny's hand-wringing, essentially demonstrating Johnny's unfitness for roasting
jokes.explanation: Roland turning the tables and doing a spot-on imitation of Johnny's nervous, apologetic energy is good character comedy. It also functions as exposition about the roast stakes.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, Roland, that's because you didn't go to school. You were homeschooled by a man named Frank who lived in a van.

jokes.text: David: 'You make me sound like a feral cat. I'm walking home.'
jokes.setup: Moira expresses surprise at seeing David 'walking the streets all by yourself'
jokes.punchline: David's indignant reframe — the phrase implies he's an abandoned street animal, not a grown adult
jokes.explanation: Economical and sharp. 'Feral cat' is an unexpectedly vivid image, and David's wounded dignity is perfect. A small gem of character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm sorry, I didn't realize you were so sensitive. What are you gonna do, cry about it? Oh wait, you already are!

jokes.text: Moira: 'Don't tell me the store is already laying off people!'
jokes.setup: David is walking home mid-afternoon
jokes.punchline: Moira's first assumption is that David's small business has already failed — cheerfully delivered, no malice intended
jokes.explanation: Very Moira — the casual confidence that the store is already failing is a perfect character note. Her obliviousness to how this lands on David is the comedic engine.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, you're gonna get eaten alive out there. You know that, right? You're gonna walk out there, and people are gonna see you, and they're gonna think, 'Who is this guy?' And then you're gonna open your mouth, and they're gonna know.

jokes.text: David explains the cheque 'is tax for the government, and not, as I assumed, a bonus for me.'
jokes.setup: Patrick sent David home to retrieve an important cheque
jokes.punchline: David's breezy confession that he genuinely thought a tax cheque was a personal bonus reveals how little he understands basic business
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-specific joke. David's former wealthy-family logic — where money randomly arrives as bonuses — bleeding into his new small-business reality is perfectly observed. The phrasing 'not, as I assumed' is elegant.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Why are you walking alone?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because apparently that's what I do now. I walk. Alone. Like some sort of... solitary figure in a Bergman film, pondering the meaninglessness of existence and my mother's inability to simply let me be.

jokes.text: Moira tells David he has 'been awfully clingy of late' and suggests he spend time with his 'peers'
jokes.setup: David wants to have dinner with his mother
jokes.punchline: Moira, the most self-absorbed woman alive, is rejecting her adult son for being 'too clingy'
jokes.explanation: The irony of Moira — a woman who demands constant attention — accusing David of clinginess is rich character comedy. Not a punchline joke but a sustained ironic beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going home because I assumed taxes were a bonus.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Turns out they're not. They're actually money I owe.

jokes.text: David trying to keep Moira away from rehearsal: 'Your choreography has been working them so hard they basically just go home and fall sleep, so actually your show is stripping me of a social life.'
jokes.setup: David is desperately covering for Patrick and Stevie, trying to explain why they can't see him
jokes.punchline: David pivots the lie into an accusation against Moira — framing her directing as the reason he has no social life
jokes.explanation: David's ability to turn a cover-up into a grievance against the person he's lying to is a beautifully petty character move. Good dramatic irony since we know he's lying.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David wants to have dinner? That's wonderful. Does he know how to cook, or is he just going to stand there looking vaguely disappointed at a plate of food?

jokes.text: Moira: 'You employ the wrong font, and you may as well bid Auf Wiedersehen to ticket sales.'
jokes.setup: Moira consents to dinner but says David must help with poster design
jokes.punchline: Moira treats font selection as an existential threat to the show's commercial viability, using German for dramatic flair
jokes.explanation: Pure Moira. 'Auf Wiedersehen' as a farewell to ticket sales is exactly how she speaks — operatic, multilingual, catastrophizing. The font anxiety is absurdly specific and the German goodbye is perfectly gratuitous. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, you really should cultivate other friendships. It's propitious to have a varied social circle.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, no one uses 'propitious' in casual conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I just did, and it was quite propitious.

jokes.text: David: 'We're both going home though! What the hell?!'
jokes.setup: Moira declines dinner, says David should be with his peers — then heads in the same direction as David
jokes.punchline: David's dawning realization that Moira is walking home too, rendering her rejection of dinner logistically absurd
jokes.explanation: A great physical/situational button on the scene. Simple but effective — the comedy comes entirely from the situation, not the words. David's exasperated 'What the hell?!' is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They're exhausted because my mother has them doing these incredibly elaborate dance numbers. She's been drilling them relentlessly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's called *art*, David. And if my dancers can't handle a few simple choreographic sequences, then perhaps they shouldn't be in show business.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I didn't [do those dishes], I just put them on the counter.'
jokes.setup: Ted says Alexis doesn't need to do those dishes
jokes.punchline: Alexis clarifies she didn't do them — she merely relocated them to the counter, which apparently doesn't count
jokes.explanation: Compact and perfectly in character. Alexis's self-image as someone who 'does things' when she is merely moving things around is delightful. Excellent economy — the joke is entirely in what she doesn't say (that this is nowhere near doing dishes).

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You cannot use that font. It's too pedestrian. It screams 'I have no taste and I shop at a strip mall.' Achtung, baby!

jokes.text: Ted: 'Emmett the tortoise is 180-years-old, so I'm sure he'll still be around in another few years.'
jokes.setup: Ted is downplaying the significance of the research opportunity, saying he can apply again
jokes.punchline: He cites a 180-year-old tortoise as evidence there's no rush — his love of animals making even his self-sacrifice adorably nerdy
jokes.explanation: Deceptively beautiful character joke. The tortoise's immortality as consolation for missing a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity is both funny and quietly heartbreaking. Only Ted could deliver this line. High character integration — this is his personality distilled perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, goodbye!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, where are you going?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm heading this way.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: ...That's the way I'm going.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I'd totally think about it, but like, my laptop's here...'
jokes.setup: Alexis is considering whether she could go with Ted to the Galapagos
jokes.punchline: Her primary logistical concern about joining a six-month research expedition to the Galapagos Islands is the location of her laptop
jokes.explanation: An exceptional character joke. Alexis's worldview reduces 'six months in the Galapagos' to a logistical laptop problem. The trailing 'like' and the non-completion of the thought are perfect delivery markers. Highly quotable and rewatchable because it reveals everything about Alexis in six words.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, actually, the Galapagos Islands are a UNESCO World Heritage Site with unique endemic species found nowhere else on Earth. They're a living laboratory of evolution, which is why Charles Darwin himself conducted his most important research there in 1835. So I would say that's a pretty good reason to go.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I once maintained a successful, semi-committed text relationship with Josh Hartnett while he was shooting Pearl Harbor.'
jokes.setup: Alexis proposes long-distance as a solution to the Galapagos dilemma
jokes.punchline: Her example of a functional long-distance relationship is a 'semi-committed text relationship' with a celebrity — delivered with total sincerity as proof of concept
jokes.explanation: A quintessential Alexis line. Every word earns its place: 'successful,' 'semi-committed,' 'text relationship,' 'Josh Hartnett,' 'while he was shooting Pearl Harbor.' The specificity is staggering. Pearl Harbor in particular is perfectly chosen — a notorious production that invites ridicule. Only Alexis would cite this as a genuine credential. One of the episode's strongest jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Look, I know it seems like a big opportunity, but I'm not going to rush into anything. Tortoises live for over 100 years, and they seem pretty happy about it. So what's the rush?

jokes.text: After the emotional conversation, Alexis announces: 'I'm gonna do those dishes.' Then: 'Babe, do you have a dishwasher?' Then: 'Okay, I'm just gonna... figure it out then.'
jokes.setup: Alexis attempts to do something domestic as a gesture of maturity after a meaningful conversation
jokes.punchline: She immediately reveals she doesn't know how to do dishes — and possibly doesn't know what a dishwasher is — then covers with cheerful confidence
jokes.explanation: Three-beat escalating gag that's very well constructed. The first beat (I'll do the dishes) signals growth; the second (do you have a dishwasher?) undercuts it; the third (I'll figure it out) is the resilient button. Deeply character-specific and earns real warmth along with the laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That's a dumb opportunity.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Actually, no, wait—this could change everything for the business.

jokes.text: Twyla asks if Alexis knows those days where you're 'just like, super in love.' Twyla: 'Hmm, not really.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is gushing about being in love, asks if Twyla knows the feeling
jokes.punchline: Twyla's flat, matter-of-fact 'Not really' — delivered without self-pity — is a quiet gut-punch of a character note
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — two words do all the work. Twyla's cheerful acceptance of her own lovelessness is one of her defining character traits, and this line captures it perfectly. The brevity is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I was in a long-distance relationship once. With this guy who was a stunt double for a stunt double on a Netflix series that got cancelled after two episodes.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Ted and I are so good at that whole give-and-take thing. Like, he gives me so much, and I have learned to take as well.'
jokes.setup: Twyla says relationships are about sacrifice; Alexis enthusiastically agrees she and Ted are great at 'give and take'
jokes.punchline: Alexis's definition of 'give and take' is that Ted gives and she takes — delivered with total sincerity, oblivious to the imbalance
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes of the episode. 'He gives me so much, and I have learned to take as well' is a masterclass in character comedy writing — the phrasing sounds like a growth statement right up until the punchline reframes it as the opposite. Alexis's complete sincerity makes it devastating. Highly quotable, perfectly structured.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So... Ted's really giving up his whole career for me?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Alexis. He's moving across the country.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Well... that's... I mean, should I do something nice for him?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know, maybe tell him you love him?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, yes. Okay. Um, anyway, so I was thinking about getting a blowout tomorrow—

jokes.text: Alexis tells Twyla she's 'like my little fairy godmother, if she wore an apron, and Celine Dion's perfume.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has just had an epiphany thanks to Twyla's gentle questioning
jokes.punchline: The 'compliment' is so specific and backhanded — an apron and Celine Dion perfume as identifiers — that it's funnier than a conventional insult
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully Alexis-coded exit line. The 'compliment' inadvertently codes Twyla as provincial and slightly sad, while Alexis means it warmly. Celine Dion's perfume is a hilariously specific and slightly tragic detail.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm going to do the dishes. It's a gesture.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I don't know if Ted even has a dishwasher. Or a sink. Or running water, for that matter.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But I'm going to try anyway.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Thanks for the chat, Twy. You're like my little fairy godmother, if she wore an apron, and Celine Dion's perfume.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has had a moment of self-realization thanks to Twyla's gentle questions
jokes.punchline: Her warm gratitude is immediately undercut by a backhanded description of Twyla as a working-class fairy godmother
jokes.explanation: A warm insult that Alexis doesn't register as an insult. Celine Dion's perfume as a class signifier is funny and specific. Alexis's oblivious affection for Twyla while simultaneously condescending to her is character gold.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Have you ever been in love?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: No, not really. I mean, I thought I was in love with Ted once, but then he started talking about soil pH levels and I realized I was just lonely.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I've got a lot of good material here, I just need some help sifting out the gold.' Ronnie: 'See, that's what I'm looking for — honest reactions.' Ronnie: 'What's that supposed to mean?' Johnny: 'See?'
jokes.setup: Johnny has gathered Ronnie and Bob to test his roast material; he says he wants 'honest reactions'
jokes.punchline: Ronnie immediately demonstrates honest (negative) reactions to everything, including Johnny calling her a 'tough audience' — proving his point instantly
jokes.explanation: A tight self-demonstrating joke: Johnny explains what he wants, Ronnie proves she's exactly that, and Johnny's 'See?' closes the loop. Good structure with quick escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I think in a relationship, it's all about give and take. You know, you give a little, you take a little.

jokes.text: Bob: 'I've been honestly reacting quite strongly to your aftershave. It's a bit too European for my taste.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is seeking honest feedback on his comedy material
jokes.punchline: Bob's 'honest reaction' is to the aftershave, not the jokes — a completely unhelpful but character-true non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: Bob's completely irrelevant aftershave complaint is good small-town character comedy. 'Too European for my taste' is a nice touch — vague and mildly xenophobic in the most benign Bob way possible.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh, Ted's so selfless. He's staying in Schitt's Creek just for me.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Wait... he's staying in Schitt's Creek? For me?

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I was flipping through the phone book the other day, and—' Ronnie: 'The phone book? How old are these?!'
jokes.setup: Johnny attempts another setup for his audience
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's horror isn't at the joke — it's at the antiquated premise material, before we even get to the punchline
jokes.explanation: Good running gag beat — Ronnie keeps intercepting Johnny before punchlines. The phone book as a dated cultural artifact is a solid observational hook, and Ronnie's indignant 'How old are THESE?!' is well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Thank you so much for the advice, Twyla. You know, you're like the Canadian version of Oprah, but less famous and with better skin.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh my gosh, thank you!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was talking about the skin thing.

jokes.text: Ronnie's roast line: 'Johnny, you gotta be short and quick. Like Roland in the bedroom.'
jokes.setup: Ronnie advises Johnny to be 'short and quick' in his delivery
jokes.punchline: She immediately uses her own advice as a demonstration — pivoting the instruction into a roast of Roland
jokes.explanation: Structurally clever: the advice IS the demonstration. Ronnie doesn't just tell Johnny to be short and quick — she shows him by landing an immediate bedroom joke. The self-exemplifying structure elevates what would otherwise be a standard roast line.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So what did you think of my material?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, I think you're wearing too much aftershave.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Now, that, that one kills every year.' Bob: 'Oh no, I love that one, is that one up for grabs?'
jokes.setup: Ronnie delivers the Roland bedroom joke
jokes.punchline: Roland cheerfully confirms his own humiliation is a perennial crowd-pleaser; Bob immediately asks to steal it
jokes.explanation: A nice character beat — Roland is so comfortable with this joke about himself that he endorses it. Bob's opportunistic 'Is that up for grabs?' adds a layer of small-town comedy charm.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, phone books used to be so thick, you couldn't even lift them with one hand.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Yeah, because they don't exist anymore, you old fossil.

jokes.text: After Ronnie leaves, Bob: 'Man, when she's on fire, look out!' Johnny, stung: 'Well, you haven't seen me on fire, Bob.'
jokes.setup: Ronnie has just efficiently demolished Johnny's material while demonstrating how it's done
jokes.punchline: Bob praises Ronnie's roasting; Johnny's wounded pride insists he hasn't shown his best yet — the audience knows better
jokes.explanation: The irony of Johnny's confidence here after being completely shredded works as a character beat. Not a strong standalone joke but adds to the episode's comedic arc.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: It's like watching someone throw a dart at a board that isn't there.

jokes.text: Moira compliments her own name being on the poster: 'That note you had about including my name on the poster as a sales tool... something I've been debating for some time now.' David: 'Your name was already on all the mock-ups. It was really more of an observation.'
jokes.setup: Moira thanks David for the 'note' about her name being a marketing asset
jokes.punchline: David reveals her name was never in question — Moira has invented a debate she won in order to credit herself
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira character joke. She has constructed an entire internal narrative in which she magnanimously agreed to use her own name, when the 'debate' never existed. David's flat correction is devastating. Very high character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: You gotta commit to the bit. Don't half-ass it.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Like, look at Roland over there doing his little song and dance. That's half-assing it right there.

jokes.text: David tries to prevent Moira from going to the rehearsal space at 7:15pm by warning it's dangerous. Moira: 'Okay David, no more True Crime TV before bed for you. I refuse to buy you another night-light!'
jokes.setup: David is desperately trying to keep Moira away from the rehearsal space where Patrick and Stevie are secretly practicing
jokes.punchline: Moira perfectly diagnoses David's anxiety as a True Crime TV habit — completely wrong reason, perfectly accurate characterization of his anxiety
jokes.explanation: Works on two levels: Moira is wrong about why David is worried, but right that he's the kind of anxious person who watches True Crime. 'I refuse to buy you another night-light' implies this has happened before, which deepens the laugh.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you can't go out at 7:15 PM. That's basically the middle of the night.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, it's 7:15 in the evening.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Exactly. That's dangerous late night hours.

jokes.text: David reacting with 'Ah!' when he arrives at the rehearsal space
jokes.setup: David has been tasked with picking up color samples so Moira doesn't go to the rehearsal space and discover the secret rehearsal
jokes.punchline: David arrives and discovers Patrick and Stevie mid-secret rehearsal — his reaction is audible shock
jokes.explanation: The situational irony payoff — the one person covering for the rehearsal walks right into it. The 'Ah!' is a non-verbal punchline to a long buildup. Works better visually than in transcript.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What is... what is happening right now?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have no idea, but the production value is surprisingly high.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Is that a spotlight? Where did they get a spotlight?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And the costumes! David, look at the costumes!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm looking. I'm looking, and I hate it.

jokes.text: David walks in on Patrick, Stevie, and Derek doing a full 'Money' production number. His reaction: 'What the hell?!'
jokes.setup: David has been covering for Patrick and Stevie all episode; he enters the rehearsal space expecting to pick up colour samples
jokes.punchline: He finds a full theatrical production number in progress — the contrast between his expectations and reality is the entire joke
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Patrick and Stevie have been secretly rehearsing a full-scale Cabaret number is a great visual payoff. David's stunned 'What the hell?!' is the perfect reaction to an absurd discovery. Works as a comedic revelation beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You deceived me. You lied to my face.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I did, and I'm sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: However... the result is rather impressive. I suppose I can forgive it.

jokes.text: Moira's verdict on the skullduggery: 'In the business of show, skullduggery, when it leads to such thrilling results, a pinch of moral corruption is a small price to pay.'
jokes.setup: Moira has caught Patrick and Stevie secretly rehearsing with outside help — a direct betrayal of her direction
jokes.punchline: Rather than punishing them, Moira delivers a philosophical justification for deception that only she could formulate
jokes.explanation: A signature Moira line. 'A pinch of moral corruption is a small price to pay' is exactly the kind of theatrical, self-serving wisdom that defines her character. It sounds like a corrupted aphorism from a Shakespearean villain, but she delivers it with complete sincerity. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific. One of the episode's best.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, collaboration is like a beautiful tapestry. You can't just pull one thread without the whole thing unraveling. It's a delicate dance of give and take, a symphony of souls intertwined in the pursuit of artistic excellence.

jokes.text: Moira on Patrick's choreography: 'I loosened the pickle jar. Derek merely popped the lid.'
jokes.setup: Patrick asks if Moira is impressed by the number she secretly helped create
jokes.punchline: Moira takes credit using a kitchen metaphor so specific and unglamorous that it's hilarious applied to show business
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's single best line. The pickle jar metaphor for theatrical collaboration is absurdly mundane from a woman who speaks in operatic abstractions. It's precise, original, and could only come from Moira — a woman who needs credit but delivers it via household metaphor. Extremely quotable. Among the sharpest lines in the season.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm finally ready to make the big decision and go.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That's great! Where are you thinking?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I've narrowed it down to two places: New York or Los Angeles.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Those are both great cities. Which one are you leaning towards?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm thinking... Switzerland.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Very impressed. Was not embarrassed at all. And I thought I would be.'
jokes.setup: Moira has just witnessed (and endorsed) their secret rehearsal
jokes.punchline: Patrick's dry, staccato relief is delivered like a corporate report — three short declarative sentences undercutting any emotional vulnerability
jokes.explanation: Patrick's deadpan delivery style is perfectly expressed here. Three staccato sentences that communicate embarrassed relief without ever admitting it. Very character-specific economy.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: We really need to get some shots.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, yes! I love shots. What are we thinking, tequila?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I was actually referring to vaccinations.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Yes, Ted Mullens, I will go to the Cayman Islands with you.' Ted: 'Uh, you mean the Galapagos?'
jokes.setup: Alexis's grand, heartfelt declaration of willingness to go with Ted
jokes.punchline: She still gets the destination wrong — now saying 'Cayman Islands' — even in her generous moment of sacrifice
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback execution. The entire episode has been building to Alexis's sacrifice, and she still can't name the destination correctly — even in her most selfless moment. The 'Cayman Islands' is the third wrong island (after Maldives), and the escalation is perfect. Shows genuine growth while still being unmistakably Alexis. One of the episode's top jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, Roland, you certainly have a lot of gut to go with.

jokes.text: Ted: 'Oh! We're gonna need shots.' Alexis: 'Yes! Let's celebrate!' Ted: 'Uh, no, no, no, I meant like, vaccinations.'
jokes.setup: Ted and Alexis are celebrating the Galapagos decision
jokes.punchline: Ted's 'shots' means vaccinations; Alexis's 'shots' means alcohol — classic misdirection that plays on their different orientations to the trip
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection but well-executed in the context of these two characters — their different energies make the misunderstanding feel organic rather than forced. The beats (Alexis's 'Yes!', Ted's gentle correction, Alexis's deflated 'Um, this is still very fresh') are all well-timed.

jokes.text: Roast line: 'Look at Bob, pretending to get it. And we all know, Gwen, he hasn't gotten it in years!'
jokes.setup: Bob laughs awkwardly at the Roland gut joke
jokes.punchline: Pivot to Bob's sex life — 'he hasn't gotten it in years' — addressed to his wife Gwen in the audience
jokes.explanation: 'Gotten it' as a double entendre directed at the wife is a roast classic. Competent and gets the Ohhhh, but conventional for the form.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I've been workshopping some real zingers. Very cutting edge material.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Cutting edge? Johnny, the only thing sharp about you is the starch in your sleeves.

jokes.text: Ronnie: 'I'm just gonna hand it over to the one who wears the pants in the Rose family. But Moira is not here right now.' [beat] 'So I'm gonna pass it over to Johnny.'
jokes.setup: Ronnie sets up her handoff by referring to the dominant Rose
jokes.punchline: The pause between 'But Moira is not here' and the redirect to Johnny lands the implication — Johnny is not the pants-wearer
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure. The pause does all the work — Ronnie builds the expectation that she'll introduce Moira, then denies it, implying Johnny is Moira's inferior. The two-sentence delivery lands the roast without explicit vulgarity. 'Those are pretty high heels to fill' called back structurally here. Sharp and well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about the food here. These 'rippers' — that's what we call them — they're absolutely devastating. Not in a good way. I ordered something that was supposed to be chicken. I'm still not entirely sure what it was. It had the texture of a hockey puck and the flavor profile of regret. My wife actually said it was the worst thing she's ever put in her mouth, and she's been married to me for thirty years.

jokes.text: Johnny's crowd-work attempt: makes fun of Jocelyn's cooking after being called out for it, then pivots to 'Bob's Garage' not 'helping' anyone — fumbling the pun
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to launch into prepared material but gets sidetracked by real crowd interactions
jokes.punchline: His improvised Bob's Garage joke ('not necessarily known for helping anyone / four helpings') is visibly clunky as he constructs it in real time
jokes.explanation: The joke works because it's intentionally bad — we're watching Johnny improvise awkwardly in real time. The fumbled wordplay ('four helpings/helping') is the point. Character-true cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, maybe if your service was better, I wouldn't need four helpings.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: My service? You've been coming to this restaurant for years.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, and I'm still waiting for the appetizer.

jokes.text: Johnny trails off mid-set: 'What was I saying?' Ronnie (from audience): 'Nothing funny so far.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is struggling through his roast set after losing his train of thought
jokes.punchline: Ronnie heckles him from the crowd with blunt accuracy
jokes.explanation: Ronnie's heckle is perfect: 'Nothing funny so far' is both objectively accurate and a killer put-down. Four words, maximum humiliation. Her delivery from the audience is the comedic engine.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Things going downhill fast? That's like comparing it to Roland becoming mayor.

jokes.text: Roland (from audience): 'Hey Johnny, come on, I've got a white flag, why don't you just start waving it? Ohhhh!'
jokes.setup: Johnny is bombing on stage
jokes.punchline: Roland's surrender metaphor — offering Johnny a white flag — is a roast pivot that works better than anything Johnny has said
jokes.explanation: Roland accidentally out-roasting Johnny is a nice situational irony. The white flag metaphor is visual and sharp for Roland. The 'Ohhhh!' confirms it lands in-universe.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Like when Roland elected himself Mayor

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Roland, may I point out that I was quiet when everyone else was up here doing their roasts.' Roland: 'I wish you were quiet right now!'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to restore order by pointing out Roland was quiet during other roasts
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately uses the logic against him — he wishes Johnny would shut up now
jokes.explanation: Clean comeback structure. Johnny sets up a logical defense, Roland turns it against him immediately. Good pacing, economical.

jokes.text: Ronnie reaction: 'Okay, I'll give him that.' — then Johnny notes it's 'got a smile out of Ronnie'
jokes.setup: Johnny lands the Roland/downhill joke
jokes.punchline: Ronnie — the harshest critic in the room — reluctantly concedes the point; Johnny immediately calls attention to it
jokes.explanation: Ronnie's reluctant approval is a good character beat. The fact that Johnny immediately points it out ('Got a smile out of Ronnie!') reveals his desperate need for validation — funny in context.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's a car-go. You know, like cargo, but it's a car. Car-go.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: At Bob's garage. Car-go to Bob's garage.

jokes.text: Johnny attempts the Roland bedroom joke: 'I'll keep things short and quick. Which reminds me of the last time I was in Roland's bedroom, um... well, I wasn't in Roland's bedroom, but the... Ah! What was it, Ronnie?' Ronnie: 'Just sit down, Johnny.' '...Well anyway, the punchline is, it was short and quick.'
jokes.setup: Johnny attempts to deliver Ronnie's 'short and quick' Roland bedroom joke
jokes.punchline: He catastrophically fumbles it — forgets the setup, asks Ronnie for help mid-joke, then delivers only the punchline without any setup
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that works as cringe comedy AND structural comedy. We saw the joke rehearsed; now we watch Johnny destroy it in real time. His asking Ronnie for help mid-delivery is a masterpiece of bombing. 'Well anyway, the punchline is, it was short and quick' — delivering a punchline without a setup — is a perfect meta-joke about his incompetence.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was just thinking, you know, a good bedroom setup is really important. The right lighting, the right—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: —no, no, I'm not doing this.

jokes.text: Johnny returns home and tells the family: 'No, I wish you guys could've been there tonight. I was really ripping into them.'
jokes.setup: Johnny returns home after a visibly difficult roast performance
jokes.punchline: His self-assessment is wildly at odds with what we witnessed — he's convinced he 'ripped into them'
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is rich — we watched him struggle, and he's narrating a victory lap. Character-true for Johnny's optimism/self-delusion balance.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You didn't kill them with comedy. You suffocated them with the pillow of your personality.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I'm sure you killed them, dear, with all the venom of a silkworm.'
jokes.setup: Johnny claims he was 'ripping into them' at the roast
jokes.punchline: Moira's backhanded 'compliment' — a silkworm is famously non-venomous, being a domesticated caterpillar — simultaneously validates and devastates
jokes.explanation: Tied with the pickle jar joke for the episode's best writing. 'The venom of a silkworm' is a devastating insult delivered as a compliment. Only Moira Rose would weaponize entomology in a casual putdown. The silkworm is precise — it's not a snake or a spider but a soft, domestic, entirely non-threatening creature. It's also condescending rather than cruel because Moira says it warmly. The vocabulary, the specificity, the character voice are all exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Take it easy? Alexis, you went to a school where the mascot was a participation trophy.

jokes.text: Johnny fires back: 'Jealousy doesn't look good on you, Moira. Much like that mirrored jumpsuit you wore to Candy Spelling's 50th!'
jokes.setup: Moira delivers the silkworm put-down
jokes.punchline: Johnny retaliates with a specific, vivid fashion attack — accusing Moira of wearing a mirrored jumpsuit to a celebrity party
jokes.explanation: Johnny's roast skills finally paying off — in the right arena (his own family). The 'mirrored jumpsuit' is a vivid, specific image that works both as a fashion attack and as a sly nod to Moira's reflective ego. Candy Spelling grounds it in their old celebrity world. Alexis's 'Oh my God, Dad!' reaction confirms it lands.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David being smart enough to get the joke, but not smart enough about his fashion choices.

jokes.text: Alexis is shocked: 'Oh my God, Dad!' Johnny to Alexis: 'Why don't you take it easy, like you did with your education?'
jokes.setup: Alexis reacts to Johnny's roast of Moira
jokes.punchline: Johnny immediately turns the roast on Alexis — her education record as a target
jokes.explanation: Johnny keeping the roast going by turning on Alexis when she reacts is great — he's found his groove. 'Take it easy like you did with your education' is a solid character-specific burn.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course he's a worthy competitor. He's competed in every business venture he's ever attempted... and lost spectacularly in each one.

jokes.text: David: 'I mean, he's not wrong.' — reaction to his own father burning Alexis
jokes.setup: Johnny roasts Alexis about her education
jokes.punchline: David immediately, calmly endorses the insult against his sister
jokes.explanation: David's deadpan fraternal betrayal — casually agreeing that his dad is correct to insult Alexis — is true to the siblings' dynamic and gets a genuine laugh. 'I mean, he's not wrong' is economically perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, David, at least I didn't name my business after myself. Oh wait, I did. But the difference is, people actually wanted to rent from Blockbuster.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's rich coming from a man who opened a store in a town where people still use VCRs.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And it's still more successful than whatever you're doing over there.

jokes.text: Moira on David: 'Smart enough to get that joke, but not smart enough to stop wearing sweaters in the middle of summer.'
jokes.setup: David endorses Johnny's burn of Alexis
jokes.punchline: Moira wheels on David — praising his intelligence only to immediately deploy it as the setup for a fashion attack
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured: Moira builds David up ('smart enough to get that joke') before using it as a ramp to attack him. The sweaters-in-summer is a real David character detail. The setup-punchline within a single sentence is elegant. Also Alexis's 'Burn, David!' adds a nice reaction beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I think we've all hit a low point.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I have a lower back tattoo.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, that's what I was referring to.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, here's why that joke didn't work.' — trying to critique Moira's burn mid-roast
jokes.setup: Moira burns David with the sweater joke; everyone reacts; David starts to analyze it
jokes.punchline: David's impulse is to workshop the joke that just burned him — his critical instinct overrides his wounded pride
jokes.explanation: Deeply character-specific. David's response to being burned is to immediately enter critique mode — his pretension and self-protection simultaneously on display. This is meta-comedy that works because it reveals character. Only David would do this.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I got it when I was like, seven.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Seven?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, at this place in Cabo. The guy didn't really ask a lot of questions.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's illegal.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know that now, Mother.

jokes.text: Moira on Johnny: 'A worthy competitor emerges. Something the good people at Blockbuster never said about Rose Video. Ha! Ha!'
jokes.setup: Moira acknowledges Johnny as a roast competitor
jokes.punchline: Immediately pivots to attacking the family's failed video business by comparing it to Blockbuster — both eventually doomed, but Blockbuster was their superior
jokes.explanation: Moira calling out Rose Video's failure by referencing Blockbuster — itself a failure — is a cut-within-a-cut. Both companies failed, but Blockbuster was at least a 'worthy competitor' in the wider world. The self-aware 'Ha! Ha!' delivery is wonderfully theatrical. Sharp, character-specific, and draws on the show's backstory.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Alexis has a back tattoo?!' — pure surprise reaction
jokes.setup: Moira reveals Alexis's back tattoo in passing
jokes.punchline: Johnny's paternal horror — he's the last to know
jokes.explanation: The parental shock reaction is simple but necessary — it's the straight-man beat that allows the final reveal to land. Johnny's exclamation mark energy here is character-true.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'It says "that's hot" in Cantonese. Got it in Hong Kong.' Moira: 'Ugh!' Alexis: 'I was 12-years-old!'
jokes.setup: Alexis is caught with a back tattoo no one knew about
jokes.punchline: The tattoo says 'that's hot' (Paris Hilton's catchphrase) in Cantonese — she got it in Hong Kong at age 12 — each detail more absurd than the last
jokes.explanation: The episode's best closing joke and arguably its funniest moment overall. Every detail lands: 'That's hot' (Paris Hilton) in Cantonese (needlessly specific language choice), gotten in Hong Kong (wealthy globe-trotting child), at age 12 (the most horrifying detail). The escalating absurdity — each clause more outrageous — combined with Alexis's complete ownership of the decision ('I was 12!') as if that explains rather than deepens the problem, is comedy writing at its best. Rivals the cargo pants 'Where? Where?' for the episode's top moment.

tmdb_overview: Johnny fills in for Moira at the annual Mayor’s Roast, as David distracts Moira from finding out that Stevie and Patrick have been taking private dance lessons.
insight: Rose family roasts themselves at 2.11 jokes-per-minute, breaking their deadpan comfort zone.
